title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a transport officer called Lena talking about a new cycling scheme in her town. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a transport officer called Lena talking about a new cycling scheme in her town. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: A transport officer called Lena explains a new community cycling scheme.
extracts.audio_script: Hello. My name’s Lena, and I work for Westbridge Council. I’m here to explain our new cycling scheme, which begins next month. People have been asking for more cycle paths for years, but it was not enough simply to guess where cyclists wanted to go. Last autumn we carried out a [9]traffic survey[/9], recording the streets people used, where cars caused difficulties, and how long journeys took. We also asked residents for ideas online. Using this information, we designed three safe routes linking homes, shops and public services.||The bicycles will be available to borrow rather than buy. There were suggestions that bikes should be collected at the railway station because visitors arrive there. However, there is little covered space at the station and staff are already busy. Our main collection point will therefore be outside the [10]library[/10], with smaller points near the park and college. Borrowing a bicycle is inexpensive, but safety is important. Every bike comes with lights and a bell; users must provide their own [11]helmet[/11], as these must fit each person properly. We also recommend carrying water for longer trips.||You may keep a bicycle for a full day. There is no need to bring a pump, as each collection point has one. The one item we cannot provide is a [12]bike lock[/12], so please take your own if you plan to stop at a café or shop. Three routes are shown on the free map. The green route is the shortest and suitable for beginners, while the red one includes a steep hill. Families who want a safe, flat ride by the river should choose the [13]blue route[/13]. Signs matching these colours will be added before the scheme opens.||Although printed maps are available, we know many people prefer their phones. A new [14]mobile app[/14] shows the routes and tells you whether bicycles are currently available at each collection point. It will also allow users to report broken lights or damaged tyres. Some residents want to learn how to deal with these problems themselves. Because of this, the council will run free [15]repair workshops[/15] on two Saturday mornings. They will cover changing a tyre and checking brakes, but not advanced repairs. We first intended to use the college workshop; instead, these sessions will be held beside the [16]shopping centre[/16], where more people can reach them easily.||We especially hope that [17]schoolchildren[/17] will use the safer routes, either with their families or, when they are older, on their journey to school. Teachers have helped us prepare simple road-safety materials. To mark the launch, we are arranging a [18]cycle festival[/18] in the park, with guided rides, helmet checks and a place where people can register for the scheme. This is not a race, so everybody can take part. Full details and application forms are on the council website, and I will now answer any questions you may have.

dialogue.speaker: lena
dialogue.text: Hello. My name’s Lena, and I work for Westbridge Council. I’m here to explain our new cycling scheme, which begins next month. People have been asking for more cycle paths for years, but it was not enough simply to guess where cyclists wanted to go. Last autumn we carried out a traffic survey, recording the streets people used, where cars caused difficulties, and how long journeys took. We also asked residents for ideas online. Using this information, we designed three safe routes linking homes, shops and public services.||The bicycles will be available to borrow rather than buy. There were suggestions that bikes should be collected at the railway station because visitors arrive there. However, there is little covered space at the station and staff are already busy. Our main collection point will therefore be outside the library, with smaller points near the park and college. Borrowing a bicycle is inexpensive, but safety is important. Every bike comes with lights and a bell; users must provide their own helmet, as these must fit each person properly. We also recommend carrying water for longer trips.||You may keep a bicycle for a full day. There is no need to bring a pump, as each collection point has one. The one item we cannot provide is a bike lock, so please take your own if you plan to stop at a café or shop. Three routes are shown on the free map. The green route is the shortest and suitable for beginners, while the red one includes a steep hill. Families who want a safe, flat ride by the river should choose the blue route. Signs matching these colours will be added before the scheme opens.||Although printed maps are available, we know many people prefer their phones. A new mobile app shows the routes and tells you whether bicycles are currently available at each collection point. It will also allow users to report broken lights or damaged tyres. Some residents want to learn how to deal with these problems themselves. Because of this, the council will run free repair workshops on two Saturday mornings. They will cover changing a tyre and checking brakes, but not advanced repairs. We first intended to use the college workshop; instead, these sessions will be held beside the shopping centre, where more people can reach them easily.||We especially hope that schoolchildren will use the safer routes, either with their families or, when they are older, on their journey to school. Teachers have helped us prepare simple road-safety materials. To mark the launch, we are arranging a cycle festival in the park, with guided rides, helmet checks and a place where people can register for the scheme. This is not a race, so everybody can take part. Full details and application forms are on the council website, and I will now answer any questions you may have.

questions.question: Route planning for the scheme used information from a (9) .
questions.explanation: Lena says the council carried out a 'traffic survey' before designing routes.

questions.question: The main bicycle collection point will be outside the (10) .
questions.explanation: Lena explains that the main collection point will be outside the 'library'.

questions.question: Each user is responsible for bringing a suitable (11) .
questions.explanation: Lena states that users must provide their own 'helmet'.

questions.question: Cyclists who stop during their trip should take a (12) with them.
questions.explanation: Lena says the scheme cannot provide a 'bike lock'.

questions.question: The route recommended for families alongside the river is the (13) .
questions.explanation: Lena recommends the 'blue route' for a flat family ride.

questions.question: Information about bicycle availability can be found on a (14) .
questions.explanation: Lena says the new 'mobile app' displays bicycle availability.

questions.question: Residents can learn simple bicycle maintenance at free (15) .
The maintenance sessions will be held next to the (16) .
questions.explanation: She says the workshops will take place beside the 'shopping centre'.

questions.explanation: Lena specifically identifies 'schoolchildren' as hoped-for users.

questions.question: The scheme will be launched with a (18) in the park.
questions.explanation: Lena says the launch event will be a 'cycle festival'.
